sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 90, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: W center green at (51-53, 39-41). Dark outline at (50-54, 50-54) with hollow center (51-53, 51-53). Need ~4 RIGHT to reach it.
- Plan: RIGHT x4 to absorb dark outline.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 6 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The W center green is now at (51-53, 51-53), which is exactly the hollow center of the dark outline c px=16 at (50-54, 50-54). But the level is not completed yet, and the dark outline is still there.
In Level 1, the green absorbed the dark outline when it moved into the same position. But here, the green is at (51-53, 51-53) and the dark outline is at (50-54, 50-54). The green is inside the dark outline but the dark outline hasn't been absorbed.
Wait, in Level 1, the green had to have the W center to absorb the dark outline by moving into it. But the absorption happened when the green's boundary matched the dark outline's boundary. Let me re-check Level 1.
In Level 1, the right green was at (30-32, 45-47) with W center, and the right dark outline was at (26-30, 44-48). When the green moved UP, it went to (27-29, 45-47) and the dark outline disappeared. So the green was inside the dark outline's boundary but not exactly matching it.
Hmm, but in Level 2, the green is at (51-53, 51-53) and the dark outline is at (50-54, 50-54). The green is a 3x3 inside a 5x5 dark outline. In Level 1, the green was a 3x3 inside a 5x5 dark outline (right dark outline at 26-30, 44-48 is 5x5, and green at 27-29, 45-47 is 3x3).
So the positions are similar. But in Level 1, the dark outline was absorbed. In Level 2, it's not. Maybe there's something different about Level 2.
Wait, let me re-read the Level 1 absorption. In Level 1, the right green had W center and moved UP to (27-29, 45-47), which absorbed the right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48). The left green had c center and moved LEFT/RIGHT to (33-35, 12-14), which absorbed the left dark outline at (33-35, 12-14).
So in Level 1, both greens absorbed their respective dark outlines. The right green had W center and the left green had c center. But the left green's c center was swapped to W center before absorbing the left dark outline.
Wait, let me re-check. In Level 1, the left green had c center initially, and I swapped it to W center before moving it to absorb the left dark outline. So both greens had W center when they absorbed their dark outlines.
In Level 2, the W center green is at (51-53, 51-53) inside the dark outline at (50-54, 50-54). But the dark outline hasn't been absorbed. Maybe the absorption requires a specific condition that I'm missing.
Let me look at the dark outline more carefully. In Level 1, the dark outline was c px=13 (charcoal, 13 pixels) with w px=9 (white, 9 pixels) inside. In Level 2, the dark outline is c px=16 (charcoal, 16 pixels) with w px=9 (white, 9 pixels) inside.
Wait, but in the current segmentation, I don't see a w px=9 inside the c px=16 dark outline. Let me check again.
Looking at the segmentation output:
- c px=16 bound=[[50, 50], [50, 54], [54, 54], [54, 50]] - this is the dark outline
- N px=8 bound=[[51, 51], [51, 53], [53, 53], [53, 51]] - this is the green inside
- W px=1 bound=[[52, 52]] - this is the W center of the green
So the green is inside the dark outline, but the dark outline is still there. In Level 1, the dark outline disappeared when the green moved into it.
Maybe the absorption in Level 2 requires a different condition. Or maybe the green needs to be a different color or size.
Let me think about this differently. In Level 1, the greens were N (light green) with either c or W centers. In Level 2, the greens are N with B (black) or W centers.
Maybe the B center greens can't absorb dark outlines, only the W center green can. And the W center green needs to match the dark outline's inner color (w) to absorb it.
But the W center green is already inside the dark outline. Maybe I need to click on the center to trigger the absorption, similar to how clicking swapped centers in Level 1.
Let me try clicking on the W center at (52, 52).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: W center green at (51-53, 51-53) inside dark outline at (50-54, 50-54). Dark outline NOT absorbed yet. In Level 1, absorption happened automatically when green moved into outline. Maybe need to click center to trigger absorption here.
Plan: Click on W center (52, 52) to try to absorb the dark outline.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# W center green at (51-53, 51-53) inside dark outline
# Try clicking on W center to trigger absorption
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 52, 'col': 52}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After click on W center:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'c') and n['pixels'] <= 32:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
